title_en: Zakat and Fasting in the Shafi'i School
content_en:
Al-Iqna's treatment of zakah follows Abu Shuja's organized presentation of the Shafi'i school's detailed and systematic approach to this central financial obligation. Ash-Shirbini's commentary fills in the legal reasoning, the evidentiary basis, and the practical applications that Abu Shuja's concise matn leaves implicit.

In the Shafi'i school, zakah is obligatory on five categories of property: gold and silver (and monetary equivalents), merchandise held for trade, livestock (camels, cattle, sheep and goats), and agricultural produce. The nisab for gold is twenty mithqals (approximately 85 grams modern weight) and for silver is two hundred dirhams (approximately 595 grams). The annual rate on both is one fortieth (two and a half percent). These thresholds derive from the hadiths of the Prophet reported by Abu Dawud and others, and the scholars of all four schools agree on them.

A distinctive Shafi'i ruling concerns zakah on agricultural produce. The Shafi'i school restricts obligatory zakah on crops to those that can be dried and stored long-term, specifically grains and dried fruits (wheat, barley, dates, raisins being the primary examples). Vegetables, fresh fruits, and most perishable produce are not subject to zakah in the Shafi'i school. This contrasts with the broader Maliki and Hanafi positions that extend zakah to more categories of agricultural produce. The Shafi'i restriction is based on the hadith of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that the Prophet did not take zakah on less than five awsuq (a volume measure), and the reasoning that the measurement by volume implies storability.

On the distribution of zakah, the Shafi'i school holds a distinctive position: ideally, the zakah must be distributed among all eight categories of recipients mentioned in Surah at-Tawbah (9:60) — or at least three individuals from each category present in the region. If some categories are absent, the zakah is distributed among those present. The Shafi'i school also requires that zakah be paid in the same region where the property is located, unless there is no eligible recipient there or a greater need exists elsewhere.

On the fast of Ramadan, Abu Shuja's text is succinct and ash-Shirbini's commentary covers the essential Shafi'i positions carefully. The intention (niyyah) for the Ramadan fast must be made before Fajr for each individual day — a point of consensus between the Shafi'i and Hanafi schools against the Maliki position that a single intention at the beginning of the month suffices. The Shafi'i school also requires that the intention include specifying it as the fast of Ramadan — a specific obligatory fast — not merely a general intention to fast.

The acts that break the fast in the Shafi'i school include: food, drink, and sexual intercourse with intention and awareness; deliberate vomiting; letting anything reach the body cavity through any open channel (including drops in the ears or nose); and the onset of menstruation or postnatal bleeding. The Shafi'i school's detailed analysis of what constitutes 'reaching the body cavity' (wusul ila al-jawf) is one of the more technically complex areas of fasting law, and ash-Shirbini works through the cases methodically.

The kaffarah for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast through sexual intercourse is the same across all schools: freeing a slave, then fasting sixty consecutive days, then feeding sixty poor people. The Shafi'i school holds that this kaffarah is only triggered by sexual intercourse, not by other deliberate violations. Intentionally eating or drinking without a valid excuse requires only qada (making up the day) without kaffarah in the predominant Shafi'i position — a significant difference from the Hanafi school.
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa dalam Mazhab Syafi'i
content_id:
Perlakuan Al-Iqna' terhadap zakat mengikuti penyajian terorganisir Abu Syuja' tentang pendekatan mazhab Syafi'i yang terperinci dan sistematis terhadap kewajiban finansial pokok ini. Syarh asy-Syirbini mengisi penalaran hukum, dasar dalil, dan penerapan praktis yang ditinggalkan secara implisit oleh matn ringkas Abu Syuja'.

Dalam mazhab Syafi'i, zakat diwajibkan atas lima kategori harta: emas dan perak (serta yang setara dengan uang), barang dagangan yang disimpan untuk diperdagangkan, ternak (unta, sapi, domba dan kambing), serta hasil pertanian. Nisab untuk emas adalah dua puluh mitsqal (sekitar 85 gram berat modern) dan untuk perak adalah dua ratus dirham (sekitar 595 gram). Tarif tahunan untuk keduanya adalah satu per empat puluh (dua setengah persen). Ambang batas ini berasal dari hadis-hadis Nabi yang diriwayatkan oleh Abu Dawud dan lainnya, dan para ulama dari keempat mazhab sepakat mengenainya.

Satu hukum khas Syafi'i berkaitan dengan zakat hasil pertanian. Mazhab Syafi'i membatasi kewajiban zakat atas tanaman pada tanaman yang dapat dikeringkan dan disimpan dalam jangka panjang, khususnya biji-bijian dan buah-buahan kering (gandum, jelai, kurma, kismis sebagai contoh utama). Sayuran, buah-buahan segar, dan sebagian besar hasil pertanian yang mudah busuk tidak dikenai zakat dalam mazhab Syafi'i. Ini berbeda dengan posisi Maliki dan Hanafi yang lebih luas yang memperluas zakat ke lebih banyak kategori hasil pertanian. Pembatasan Syafi'i didasarkan pada hadis Abu Sa'id al-Khudri bahwa Nabi tidak mengambil zakat dari kurang dari lima awsuq (ukuran volume), dengan alasan bahwa pengukuran berdasarkan volume mengimplikasikan kemampuan penyimpanan.

Dalam distribusi zakat, mazhab Syafi'i memiliki posisi khas: idealnya, zakat harus didistribusikan kepada semua delapan golongan penerima yang disebutkan dalam Surah at-Taubah (9:60) — atau setidaknya tiga individu dari setiap golongan yang ada di wilayah tersebut. Jika beberapa golongan tidak ada, zakat didistribusikan kepada yang ada. Mazhab Syafi'i juga mengharuskan zakat dibayarkan di wilayah yang sama di mana harta tersebut berada, kecuali jika tidak ada penerima yang berhak di sana atau ada kebutuhan yang lebih besar di tempat lain.

Dalam hal puasa Ramadan, teks Abu Syuja' singkat dan syarh asy-Syirbini mencakup posisi-posisi Syafi'i yang esensial dengan cermat. Niat untuk puasa Ramadan harus dilakukan sebelum Subuh untuk setiap hari secara individual — sebuah titik kesepakatan antara mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanafi yang bertentangan dengan posisi Maliki yang menganggap satu niat di awal bulan sudah cukup. Mazhab Syafi'i juga mengharuskan bahwa niat mencakup penetapan sebagai puasa Ramadan — puasa wajib tertentu — bukan sekadar niat puasa secara umum.

Perbuatan-perbuatan yang membatalkan puasa dalam mazhab Syafi'i meliputi: makan, minum, dan hubungan seksual dengan sengaja dan sadar; muntah dengan sengaja; membiarkan apapun mencapai rongga tubuh melalui saluran terbuka manapun (termasuk tetes di telinga atau hidung); dan timbulnya haid atau nifas. Analisis terperinci mazhab Syafi'i tentang apa yang merupakan 'mencapai rongga tubuh' (wushul ila al-jawf) adalah salah satu area hukum puasa yang lebih kompleks secara teknis, dan asy-Syirbini mengerjakan kasus-kasus tersebut secara metodis.

Kafarat karena sengaja membatalkan puasa Ramadan dengan hubungan seksual adalah sama di semua mazhab: memerdekakan budak, kemudian berpuasa enam puluh hari berturut-turut, kemudian memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin. Mazhab Syafi'i berpendapat bahwa kafarat ini hanya dipicu oleh hubungan seksual, bukan oleh pelanggaran sengaja lainnya. Sengaja makan atau minum tanpa alasan yang valid hanya memerlukan qadha (mengganti hari) tanpa kafarat dalam posisi Syafi'i yang dominan — sebuah perbedaan signifikan dari mazhab Hanafi.
content_ar: Al-Iqna's treatment of الزكاة follows Abu Shuja's organized presentation of the الشافعي school's detailed and systematic approach to this المحوري financial الالتزام. Ash-Shirbini's commentary fills in the legal reasoning, the evidentiary basis, and the practical applications that Abu Shuja's concise المتن leaves implicit. In the الشافعي school, الزكاة is obligatory on five categories of property: gold and silver (and monetary equivalents), merchandise held for trade, livestock (camels, cattle, sheep and goats), and agricultural produce. The nisab for gold is twenty mithqals (approximately 85 grams modern weight) and for silver is two hundred dirhams (approximately 595 grams). The annual rate on both is one fortieth (two and a half percent). These thresholds derive from the hadiths of النبي ﷺ reported by أبو داود and others, and the العلماء of all four schools agree on them. A distinctive الشافعي ruling concerns الزكاة on agricultural produce. The الشافعي school restricts obligatory الزكاة on crops to those that can be dried and stored long-term, specifically grains and dried fruits (wheat, barley, dates, raisins being the الأساسي examples). Vegetables, fresh fruits, and most perishable produce are not subject to الزكاة in the الشافعي school. This contrasts with the broader المالكي and الحنفي positions that extend الزكاة to more categories of agricultural produce. The الشافعي restriction is based on the الحديث of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that النبي ﷺ did not take الزكاة on less than five awsuq (a volume measure), and the reasoning that the measurement by volume implies storability. On the distribution of الزكاة, the الشافعي school holds a distinctive position: ideally, the الزكاة must be distributed among all eight categories of recipients mentioned in السورة at-التوبة (9:60) — or at least three individuals from each category present in the region. إذا some categories are absent, the الزكاة is distributed among those present. The الشافعي school أيضاً requires that الزكاة be paid in the same region where the property is located, إلا إذا there is no eligible recipient there or a greater need exists elsewhere. On the fast of Ramadan, Abu Shuja's text is succinct and ash-Shirbini's commentary covers the الجوهري الشافعي positions carefully. The intention (niyyah) for the Ramadan fast must be made قبل Fajr for each individual day — a point of consensus between the الشافعي and الحنفي schools against the المالكي position that a single intention at the beginning of the month suffices. The الشافعي school أيضاً requires that the intention include specifying it as the fast of Ramadan — a specific obligatory fast — not merely a general intention to fast. The acts that break the fast in the الشافعي school include: food, drink, and sexual intercourse with intention and awareness; deliberate vomiting; letting anything reach the الجسد cavity through any open channel (بما في ذلك drops in the ears or nose); and the onset of menstruation
